documentation:
![Red Bird](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Miksus/red-bird/master/docs/logo.svg)


# Repository Patterns for Python
> Generic database implemetation for SQL, MongoDB and in-memory lists

---

[![Pypi version](https://badgen.net/pypi/v/redbird)](https://pypi.org/project/redbird/)
[![build](https://github.com/Miksus/red-bird/actions/workflows/main.yml/badge.svg?branch=master)](https://github.com/Miksus/red-bird/actions/workflows/main.yml)
[![codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/Miksus/red-bird/branch/master/graph/badge.svg?token=GVBWCKHL1N)](https://codecov.io/gh/Miksus/red-bird)
[![Documentation Status](https://readthedocs.org/projects/red-bird/badge/?version=latest)](https://red-bird.readthedocs.io)
[![PyPI pyversions](https://badgen.net/pypi/python/redbird)](https://pypi.org/project/redbird/)

Repository pattern is a technique to abstract the data access from
the domain/business logic. In other words, it decouples the database 
access from the application code. The aim is that the code runs the 
same regardless if the data is stored to an SQL database, NoSQL 
database, file or even as an in-memory list.

Read more about the repository patterns in the [official documentation](https://red-bird.readthedocs.io).

## Why?

Repository pattern has several benefits over embedding the 
database access to the application:

- Faster prototyping and development
- Easier to migrate the database
- More readable code, Pythonic
- Unit testing and testing in general is safer and easier 

## Features

Main features:

- Support for Pydantic models for data validation
- Identical way to create, read, update and delete (CRUD)
- Pythonic and simple syntax
- Support for more complex queries (greater than, not equal, less than etc.)

Supported repositories:

- SQL
- MongoDB
- In-memory (Python list)
- JSON files
- CSV file

## Examples

First, we create a simple repo:

```python
from redbird.repos import MemoryRepo
repo = MemoryRepo()
```

> Note: the following examples work on any repository, not just in-memory repository.

Adding/creating items:

```python
repo.add({"name": "Anna", "nationality": "British"})
```

Reading items:

```python
repo.filter_by(name="Anna").all()
```

Updating items:

```python
repo.filter_by(name="Anna").update(nationality="Finnish")
```

Deleting items:

```python
repo.filter_by(name="Anna").delete()
```

See more from the [official documentation](https://red-bird.readthedocs.io).

## Author

* **Mikael Koli** - [Miksus](https://github.com/Miksus) - koli.mikael@gmail.com

classes.name: redbird.repos.memory.MemoryRepo
documentation:
Memory repository

This is a repository which operates in memory.
Useful for unit tests and prototyping.

# Parameters

collection

: The collection

model

: Class of an item in the repository.
  Commonly dict or subclass of Pydantic
  BaseModel. By default dict

id_field

: Attribute or key that identifies each item
  in the repository.

field_access

: How to access a field in an item. Either
  by attribute ('attr') or key ('item').
  By default guessed from the model.

query

: Query model of the repository.

errors_query

: Whether to raise an exception, warn or discard
  the item in case of validation error in
  converting data to the item model from
  the repository. By default raise

# Examples

```python
repo = MemoryRepo()
```

```python
repo = MemoryRepo(collection=[
    {"car_type": "van", "color": "red"},
    {"car_type": "truck", "color": "red"}
])
```

classes.name: redbird.repos.json.JSONDirectoryRepo
documentation:
JSON directory repository

This repository represents a directory which
contains JSON files. Each item represents a
file and the `id_field` is the stem of the
file.

# Parameters

path

: Path to the repository directory

model

: Class of an item in the repository.
  Commonly dict or subclass of Pydantic
  BaseModel. By default dict

id_field

: Attribute or key that identifies each item
  in the repository.

field_access

: How to access a field in an item. Either
  by attribute ('attr') or key ('item').
  By default guessed from the model.

query

: Query model of the repository.

errors_query

: Whether to raise an exception, warn or discard
  the item in case of validation error in
  converting data to the item model from
  the repository. By default raise

kwds_json_load

: Keyword arguments passed to `json.load`.

kwds_json_dump

: Keyword arguments passed to `json.dump`.
  Useful for prettifying the files

# Examples

```python
repo = JSONDirectoryRepo(path="path/to/repo", id_field="id")
```

classes.name: redbird.repos.mongo.MongoSession

methods.name: redbird.repos.mongo.MongoSession.remove
documentation:
Remove the connection

Similar in some ways to SQLAlchemy's Session.remove.
This method will close the clients and remove them.
Next time the client is accessed, a new client is 
created. Meant to be safe for multi-threaded apps.

MongoDB session

Similar as SQLAlchemy Session but works slightly 
different due to technical details

MongoClient is analogous to sqlalchemy.engine.Engine but 
MongoClient is not fork safe: https://pymongo.readthedocs.io/en/stable/faq.html#is-pymongo-fork-safe
SQLAlchemy Engine is fork safe if no connections are made.

This object is fork safe if no clients are open (call remove to close all of them).

classes.name: redbird.repos.mongo.MongoRepo

methods.name: redbird.repos.mongo.MongoRepo.query_data
documentation: Query based

MongoDB Repository

# Parameters

uri

: Connection string to the database.
  Pass uri, client or session.

client

: MongoDB client for the connection.
  Pass uri, client or session.

model

: Class of an item in the repository.
  Commonly dict or subclass of Pydantic
  BaseModel. By default dict

id_field

: Attribute or key that identifies each item
  in the repository.

field_access

: How to access a field in an item. Either
  by attribute ('attr') or key ('item').
  By default guessed from the model.

query

: Query model of the repository.

errors_query

: Whether to raise an exception, warn or discard
  the item in case of validation error in
  converting data to the item model from
  the repository. By default raise

session

: A MongoDB session object that should
  have at least `client` attribute.
  Pass uri, client or session.

# Examples

```python
repo = MongoRepo(uri="mongodb://localhost:27017/mydb?authSource=admin", collection="mycol")
```

```python
repo = MongoRepo(uri="mongodb://localhost:27017", database="mydb", collection="mycol")
```

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
repo = MongoRepo(client=MongoClient("mongodb://localhost:27017"))
```

classes.name: redbird.repos.csv.CSVFileRepo
documentation:
CSV file repository

This repository has a CSV (comma-separated values)
file as a data store. Each item represents a row
in the file.

# Parameters

filename

: The repository file

fieldnames

: Names of the columns in the CSV file.
  If unspecified, model's fields are used
  instead

model

: Class of an item in the repository.
  Commonly dict or subclass of Pydantic
  BaseModel. By default dict

id_field

: Attribute or key that identifies each item
  in the repository.

field_access

: How to access a field in an item. Either
  by attribute ('attr') or key ('item').
  By default guessed from the model.

query

: Query model of the repository.

errors_query

: Whether to raise an exception, warn or discard
  the item in case of validation error in
  converting data to the item model from
  the repository. By default raise

kwds_csv

: Keyword arguments used to create
  `csv.DictWriter` and `csv.DictReader`

# Examples

```python
repo = CSVFileRepo(filepath="path/to/repo", fieldnames=['id', 'name', 'age'])
```

classes.name: redbird.repos.rest.RESTRepo
documentation:
REST API Repository

# Parameters

url

: Base URL for the API. Should not contain
  query parameters or the item ID. The full
  URL will be "\{url}/\{id}" if a single item
  is searched, "\{url}?\{param}=\{value}" if
  the endpoint is queried.

model

: Class of an item in the repository.
  Commonly dict or subclass of Pydantic
  BaseModel. By default dict

id_field

: Attribute or key that identifies each item
  in the repository.

field_access

: How to access a field in an item. Either
  by attribute ('attr') or key ('item').
  By default guessed from the model.

query

: Query model of the repository.

errors_query

: Whether to raise an exception, warn or discard
  the item in case of validation error in
  converting data to the item model from
  the repository. By default raise

headers

: HTTP Headers (for example authentication)

url_params

: Additional query parameters passed to every
  request.

result

: Where the list of items is found from the output.

# Examples

```python
repo = MemoryRepo(url="http://example.com/api")
```

For services requiring authentication via headers:
.. code-block:: python

> token = "1234567890"
> repo = RESTRepo(url="<http://example.com/api>", headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer \{token}"})

You may also supply some URL parameters for every query:

```python
repo = RESTRepo(url="http://example.com/api", url_params={"fields": "car_type,car_model,registration_number"})
```

classes.name: redbird.repos.sqlalchemy.SQLRepo
documentation:
SQL Repository

# Parameters

conn_string

: Connection string to the database.
  Pass either conn_string, engine or session if
  model_orm is not defined.

engine

: SQLAlchemy engine to connect the database.
  Pass either conn_string, engine or session if
  model_orm is not defined.

model

: Class of an item in the repository.
  Commonly dict or subclass of Pydantic
  BaseModel. By default dict

id_field

: Attribute or key that identifies each item
  in the repository.

field_access

: How to access a field in an item. Either
  by attribute ('attr') or key ('item').
  By default guessed from the model.

query

: Query model of the repository.

errors_query

: Whether to raise an exception, warn or discard
  the item in case of validation error in
  converting data to the item model from
  the repository. By default raise

model_orm

: Subclass of SQL Alchemy representation of the item.
  This is the class that is operated behind the scenes.

table

: Table name where the items lies. Should only be given
  if no model_orm specified.

session

: Connection session to the database.
  Pass either conn_string, engine or session if
  model_orm is not defined.

# Examples

```python
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from redbird.repos import SQLRepo

engine = create_engine('sqlite://')
repo = SQLRepo(engine=engine, table="my_table")
```

You may also supply a session:

```python
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session
from redbird.repos import SQLRepo

engine = create_engine('sqlite://')
session = Session(engine)
repo = SQLRepo(session=session, table="my_table")
```

Using ORM model:

```python
from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base

Base = declarative_base()

class Car(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'my_table'
    color = Column(String, primary_key=True)
    car_type = Column(String)
    milage = Column(Integer)

from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from redbird.repos import SQLRepo

engine = create_engine('sqlite://')
repo = SQLRepo(model_orm=Car, engine=engine)
```

Using ORM model and reflect Pydantic Model:

```python
from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base

Base = declarative_base()

class Car(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'my_table'
    color = Column(String, primary_key=True)
    car_type = Column(String)
    milage = Column(Integer)

from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from redbird.repos import SQLRepo

engine = create_engine('sqlite://')
repo = SQLRepo(model_orm=Car, reflect_model=True, engine=engine)
```

Using ORM model and Pydantic Model:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base

Base = declarative_base()

class CarORM(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'my_table'
    color = Column(String, primary_key=True)
    car_type = Column(String)
    milage = Column(Integer)

class Car(BaseModel):
    id: str
    name: str
    age: int

from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from redbird.repos import SQLRepo

engine = create_engine('sqlite://')
repo = SQLRepo(model=Car, model_orm=CarORM, engine=engine)
```

classes.name: redbird.base.BaseResult
documentation:
Abstract filter result

Result classes add additional alchemy
to Red Bird providing convenient ways
to read, modify or delete data. 

Subclass of BaseRepo should also have custom
subclass of BaseResult as cls_result attribute.

Upsert item to the repository

# Examples

```python

```

methods.name: redbird.base.BaseRepo.get_field_value
documentation:
Utility method to get key's value from an item

If item's fields are accessed via attribute,
getattr is used. If fields are accessed via 
items, getitem is used.

methods.name: redbird.base.BaseRepo.set_field_value
documentation:
Utility method to set field's value in an item

If item's fields are accessed via attribute,
setattr is used. If fields are accessed via 
items, setitem is used.

Abstract Repository

Base class for the repository pattern.

Parameters
----------
model : Type
    Class of an item in the repository.
    Commonly dict or subclass of Pydantic
    BaseModel. By default dict
id_field : str, optional
    Attribute or key that identifies each item
    in the repository.
field_access : {'attr', 'key'}, optional
    How to access a field in an item. Either
    by attribute ('attr') or key ('item').
    By default guessed from the model.
query : Type, optional
    Query model of the repository.
errors_query : {'raise', 'warn', 'discard'}
    Whether to raise an exception, warn or discard
    the item in case of validation error in 
    converting data to the item model from
    the repository. By default raise

classes.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.select
documentation:
Read the database table using a query.

# Parameters

qry

: Query to filter the data. The argument can take various forms:

  - `str`: Query is considered to be raw SQL
  - `dict`: Query is considered to be column-filter pairs.
    The pairs are combined using AND operator. If the filter
    is Operation, it is turned to corresponing SQLAlchemy expression.
    Else, the filter is considered to be an equal operator.
  - sqlalchemy expression: The query is considered to be the *where*
    clause of the select query.

  If not given, all rows are returned.

columns

: List of columns to return. By default returns all columns.

parameters

: Parameters for the query. If the query is as raw SQL,
  they should be set in the query with `:myparam`, ie.
  `select * from table where col = :myparam`.

# Returns

List of dicts

: Found rows as dicts.

# Examples

Select all rows:

```python
table.select()
```

Select using raw SQL:

```python
table.select("SELECT * FROM mytable")
```

Select using dictionary:

```python
table.select({"column_1": "a value", "column_2": 10})
```

:::{note}
The above is same as:

```sql
SELECT *
FROM mytable
WHERE column_1 = 'a value' AND column_2 = 10
```
:::

Select using SQLAlchemy expressions:

```python
from sqlalchemy import Column
table.select((Column("column_1") == "a value") & (Column("column_2") == 10))
```

:::{note}
The above is same as:

```sql
SELECT *
FROM mytable
WHERE column_1 = 'a value' AND column_2 = 10
```
:::

Select and return specific column(s):

```python
table.select(columns=["column_1", "column_2"])
```

:::{note}
The above is same as:

```sql
SELECT column_1, column_2
FROM mytable
```
:::

Select using raw strings and SQL parameters:

```python
table.select(
    "SELECT * FROM mytable WHERE column_1 = :myparam",
    parameters={"myparam": "a value"}
)
```

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.insert
documentation:
Insert data to the database.

# Parameters

data

: Data to be inserted.

# Examples

Insert a single row:

```python
table.insert({"column_1": "a", "column_2": 1})
```

Insert multiple rows:

```python
table.insert([
    {"column_1": "a", "column_2": 1},
    {"column_1": "b", "column_2": 2},
])
```

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.delete
documentation:
Delete row(s) from the table.

# Parameters

where

: Where clause to delete data.

# Returns

int

: Count of rows deleted.

# Examples

Delete using dictionary:

```python
table.delete({"column_1": "a", "column_2": 1})
```

:::{note}
The above is same as:

```sql
DELETE FROM mytable
WHERE column_1 = 'a' AND column_2 = 1
```
:::

Delete using SQL expressions:

```python
from sqlalchemy import Column
table.delete((Column("column_1") == "a") & (Column("column_2") == 1))
```

:::{note}
The above is same as:

```sql
DELETE FROM mytable
WHERE column_1 = 'a' AND column_2 = 1
```
:::

Delete all:

```python
table.delete({})
```

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.update
documentation:
Update row(s) in the table.

# Parameters

where

: Where clause to update rows.

values

: Column-value pairs to update the
  rows matching the where clause.

# Returns

int

: Count of rows updated.

# Examples

Update using dicts:

```python
table.update({"column_1": "a", "column_2": 1}, {"column_3": "new value"})
```

:::{note}
The above is same as:

```sql
UPDATE mytable
SET column_3='new value'
WHERE column_1 = 'a' and column_2 = 1
```
:::

Update using expressions:

```python
from sqlalchemy import Column
table.update((Column("column_1") == "a") & (Column("column_2") == 1), {"column_3": "new value"})
```

:::{note}
The above is same as:

```sql
UPDATE mytable
SET column_3='new value'
WHERE column_1 = 'a' and column_2 = 1
```
:::

Update all:

```python
table.update({}, {"column_3": "new value"})
```

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.count
documentation:
Count the number of rows.

# Parameters

where

: Where clause to be satisfied for counting the rows.

# Returns

int

: Count of rows (satisfying the where clause).

# Examples

Count based on dict:

```python
table.count({"column_1": "a", "column_2": 1})
```

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.exists

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.create
documentation:
Create the table

# Parameters

columns

: Columns to be created.

exist_ok

: If false (default), an exception is raised.

# Examples

There are various ways to call this method:

- Using list of columns (all of the)

Create a table with columns `column_1`, `column_2` and `column_3`
(all of them are textual columns):

```python
table.create(["column_1", "column_2", "column_3"])
```

Create a table with columns `column_1`, `column_2` and `column_3`
with varying data types:

```python
import datetime
table.create({"column_1": str, "column_2": int, "column_3": datetime.datetime})
```

Create a table with columns `column_1`, `column_2` and `column_3`
using SQLAlchemy columns:

```python
from sqlalchemy import Column, String, Integer, DateTime
table.create([
    Column("column_1", type_=String()),
    Column("column_2", type_=Integer()),
    Column("column_3", type_=DateTime())
])
```

Create a table with columns `column_1`, `column_2` and `column_3`
using list of dicts:

```python
from sqlalchemy import DateTime
table.create([
    {"name": "column_1", "type_": str},
    {"name": "column_2", "type_": int},
    {"name": "column_3", "type_": DateTime()},
])
```

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.execute
documentation:
Execute SQL statement or raw SQL.

Parameters
----------
*args : tuple
    Passed directly to sqlalchemy.Connection.execute.
**args : dict
    Passed directly to sqlalchemy.Connection.execute.

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.open_transaction
documentation:
Open a transaction.

# Examples

```python
from redbird.sql import Table
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

table = Table("mytable", bind=create_engine(...))

# Open the transaction
transaction = table.open_transaction()

# Perform operations
transaction.insert({"col_1": "a", "col_2": "b"})
transaction.delete({"col_2": "c"})

# Commit or rollback the changes
if successful:
    transaction.commit()
else:
    transaction.rollback()
```

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table.transaction
documentation:
Open a transaction context.

If an error is raised inside the with block,
the changes are rollbacked. Else they are
commited.

# Examples

```python
from redbird.sql import Table
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

table = Table("mytable", bind=create_engine(...))

with table.transaction() as trans:

    # Perform operations
    trans.insert({"col_1": "a", "col_2": "b"})
    trans.delete({"col_2": "c"})
```

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table._trans_ctx.__init__
documentation: Utility for transaction context manager

methods.name: redbird.sql.expressions.Table._exec_ctx.__init__
documentation: Utility for execution context manager

SQL Table

Similar to `sqlalchemy.Table` except this class always
points to a specific SQL table in a specific database
(which may not yet exist) and provides further abstraction.

# Attributes

name

: Name of the table.

bind

: SQLAlchemy engine or connection.

# Examples

```python
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from redbird.sql import Table

table = Table("mytable", bind=create_engine("sqlite://"))
```

classes.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_data
documentation:
Query (read) the data store and return raw data

Override this or {func}`~redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_items` method.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

# Returns

iterable (any)

: Iterable of raw data that is converted to an item using {func}`~redbird.base.BaseRepo.data_to_item`

# Examples

Used in following cases:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").all()
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_items
documentation:
Query (read) the data store and return items

Override this or {func}`~redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_data` method.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

# Returns

iterable (`model`)

: Items that are instances of the class in the `model` attibute.
  Typically dicts or instances of subclassed Pydantic BaseModel

# Examples

Used in following cases:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").all()
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_update
documentation:
Update the result of the query with given values

Override this method.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

# Examples

Used in following cases:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").update(color="blue")
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_delete
documentation:
Delete the result of the query

Override this method.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

# Examples

Used in following cases:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").delete()
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_read_first
documentation:
Query the first item

You may override this method. By default,
the first item returned by {class}`TemplateRepo.query_read`
is returned.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

# Examples

Used in the following case:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").first()
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_read_limit
documentation:
Query the first n items

You may override this method. By default,
the N first items returned by {class}`TemplateRepo.query_read`
are returned.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

n

: Number of items to return

# Examples

Used in the following case:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").limit(3)
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_read_last
documentation:
Query the last item

You may override this method. By default,
the last item returned by {class}`TemplateRepo.query_read`
is returned.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

# Examples

Used in the following case:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").last()
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_replace
documentation:
Replace the items with given values using given query

You may override this method. By default,
the result of the query is deleted and an
item from the values is generated.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

values

: Values to replace the items' existing
  values with

# Examples

Used in the following case:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").replace(color="blue")
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.query_count
documentation:
Count the items returned by the query

You may override this method. By default,
the items returned by {class}`TemplateRepo.query_read`
are counted.

# Parameters

query

: Repository query, by default dict.

# Examples

Used in the following case:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red").count()
```

methods.name: redbird.templates.TemplateRepo.format_query
documentation:
Format the query to a format suitable by the repository

You may override this method. By default,
the query is as dictionary.

# Parameters

query

: Query to reformat

# Examples

Used in the following case:

```python
repo.filter_by(color="red")
```

Template repository for easy subclassing

# Parameters

model

: Class of an item in the repository.
  Commonly dict or subclass of Pydantic
  BaseModel. By default dict

id_field

: Attribute or key that identifies each item
  in the repository.

field_access

: How to access a field in an item. Either
  by attribute ('attr') or key ('item').
  By default guessed from the model.

query

: Query model of the repository.

errors_query

: Whether to raise an exception, warn or discard
  the item inn case of validation error in
  converting data to the item model from
  the repository. By default raise

# Examples

```python
class MyRepo(TemplateRepo):

    def insert(self, item):
        # Insert item to the data store
        ...

    def query_read(self, query: dict):
        # Get data from repository
        for item in ...:
            yield item

    def query_update(self, query: dict, values: dict):
        # Update items with values matcing the query
        ...

    def query_delete(self, query: dict):
        # Delete items matcing the query
        ...

    def item_to_data(self, item):
        # Convert item to type that is understandable
        # by the repository's data store
        ...
        return data
```
